name: Angola
tagline: From the kingdoms of Kongo, Ndongo and Matamba and Queen Njinga's long resistance, through centuries as a heartland of the Atlantic slave trade and a slow colonial conquest, to a hard-won independence and an oil-rich modern republic forged out of decades of war.
summary: The land that became Angola was home to powerful Central African polities long before European arrival: the great Kingdom of Kongo, whose influence reached the northern reaches of present-day Angola, and the Kimbundu-speaking kingdoms of Ndongo and Matamba, where in the seventeenth century Queen Njinga (Nzinga) Mbande waged a celebrated decades-long resistance against Portuguese encroachment. Further inland lay the Ovimbundu kingdoms of the central highlands and the Lunda states of the east. After Paulo Dias de Novais founded Luanda in 1576, Angola became, over the following centuries, one of the principal sources of enslaved Africans for the Atlantic trade, above all to Portuguese Brazil. The Portuguese conquest of the interior was slow and bloody, completed only in the early twentieth century. Anti-colonial revolt broke out in 1961, fought by the rival movements MPLA, FNLA and UNITA, and Angola won independence on 11 November 1975 under the MPLA's Agostinho Neto. That triumph gave way at once to a long Cold War-era civil war between the MPLA and Jonas Savimbi's UNITA, drawing in Cuban, Soviet, South African and United States forces, which ended only with Savimbi's death in 2002. Modern Angola, rebuilt on its vast oil wealth, has remained under MPLA rule.

founding.label: Independence from Portugal
founding.detail: Angola became a sovereign state on 11 November 1975, when, following the 1974 Carnation Revolution in Lisbon and the collapse of Portugal's colonial empire, the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA) proclaimed the People's Republic of Angola in Luanda under its leader Agostinho Neto, who became the country's first president. That moment rested atop a deep precolonial past — the Kingdom of Kongo to the north, the Kimbundu kingdoms of Ndongo and Matamba where Queen Njinga resisted the Portuguese in the seventeenth century, and the Ovimbundu and Lunda states of the interior. Portuguese presence began with the founding of Luanda in 1576, after which Angola became a major source of enslaved Africans for the Atlantic trade, especially to Brazil; the conquest of the interior was only completed in the early twentieth century. Independence in 1975 was contested between three rival liberation movements and ushered in a civil war that would last until 2002.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From Portugal on 11 November 1975, under the MPLA's Agostinho Neto

quickFacts.label: Colonial foothold
quickFacts.value: Luanda founded in 1576 by Paulo Dias de Novais as São Paulo da Assunção de Loanda

quickFacts.label: Precolonial states
quickFacts.value: Kingdom of Kongo, the kingdoms of Ndongo and Matamba, and the Ovimbundu and Lunda kingdoms

quickFacts.label: Atlantic slave trade
quickFacts.value: A principal source of enslaved Africans, above all to Brazil, from the late 16th to the 19th century

quickFacts.label: Civil war
quickFacts.value: MPLA versus UNITA from 1975 until Jonas Savimbi's death in 2002

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: An OPEC member and one of Africa's leading oil producers

eras.title: Kongo, Ndongo and the kingdoms of Central Africa
eras.standfirst: Before the Portuguese arrived, the region was shaped by the great Kingdom of Kongo to the north and the Kimbundu kingdoms of Ndongo and Matamba, with the Ovimbundu and Lunda peoples taking form in the interior.
body: By the late medieval period, Central Africa held sophisticated states. The most powerful was the Kingdom of Kongo, centred north of the Congo River but whose authority and cultural reach extended into the northern parts of present-day Angola. When Portuguese caravels reached the Kongo coast in 1483, they encountered a complex, centralised monarchy; its ruler Nzinga a Nkuwu converted to Christianity, and under his son Afonso I the kingdom adopted Catholicism, literacy and diplomatic ties with Lisbon and Rome.

South of Kongo lived the Mbundu (Ambundu) peoples, speakers of the Bantu language Kimbundu, organised under a ruler titled the ngola — a word from which the name 'Angola' derives. Their principal state was the Kingdom of Ndongo, according to oral tradition founded by a migrant from Kongo and at first a vassal of the larger kingdom before asserting its independence. To its east lay Matamba, another Kimbundu polity that would later become famous as Queen Njinga's stronghold.

Deeper in the interior, further societies were taking shape. In the central highlands the Ovimbundu formed a cluster of kingdoms that would later dominate the region's caravan trade, while in the east the Lunda states extended a far-reaching commercial and political network across the savanna. These inland peoples would long remain beyond Portuguese reach, drawn into the wider world chiefly through the trade routes that linked the interior to the Atlantic coast.

events.title: Portuguese reach the Kingdom of Kongo
events.summary: The navigator Diogo Cão reached the mouth of the Congo River and made contact with the Kingdom of Kongo, opening relations between this powerful Central African monarchy and Portugal.

events.title: Kongo adopts Christianity
events.summary: The Kongo ruler was baptised and, under his son Afonso I, the kingdom embraced Catholicism, literacy and sustained diplomatic ties with Portugal and the papacy.

events.title: Rise of Ndongo under the ngola
events.summary: The Kimbundu-speaking Kingdom of Ndongo grew in strength under its ruler the ngola, asserting independence from Kongo; the title would give the future colony its name.

events.title: Ovimbundu and Lunda states take shape
events.summary: In the central highlands the Ovimbundu kingdoms emerged, while in the east the Lunda states built a wide commercial and political network across the interior savanna.

pullquote.text: The name 'Angola' comes from the ngola, the royal title of the Mbundu rulers of Ndongo.

eras.title: Luanda, Queen Njinga and the Atlantic slave trade
eras.standfirst: The Portuguese founded Luanda in 1576 and pressed inland for captives, provoking the long resistance of Queen Njinga of Ndongo and Matamba, as Angola became a heartland of the Atlantic slave trade to Brazil.
body: In 1576 the Portuguese captain Paulo Dias de Novais founded Luanda, originally São Paulo da Assunção de Loanda, on what was Atlantic Africa's finest natural harbour. Securing access to captives spurred the colony's growth, and Luanda became the centre of a brutal commerce: from the late sixteenth century the Portuguese waged war on the Kingdom of Ndongo to feed an ever-growing demand for enslaved labour, above all in Brazil. By the late sixteenth century, thousands of enslaved people a year were already being shipped from Angola.

Resistance crystallised in the figure of Njinga (Nzinga) Mbande, born around 1583. After a diplomatic embassy to the Portuguese in Luanda in 1621, during which she was baptised, she became ruler of Ndongo in 1624. When the Portuguese drove her out, she rebuilt her power base in the eastern kingdom of Matamba from 1631, allied at times with the Dutch who briefly held Luanda from 1641 to 1648, and fought the Portuguese in campaigns that continued almost yearly for decades. She concluded peace with Portugal in 1656 and died in 1663, remembered as a symbol of African resistance and as 'Mother of Angola'.

Over the following centuries Angola became one of the principal sources of enslaved Africans for the entire Atlantic system. Of the roughly twelve and a half million people deported across the Atlantic, scholars estimate that close to half came from West Central Africa, the region centred on Angola, with Luanda the single largest slave-trading port and Benguela, founded in 1617, a second major outlet. The overwhelming destination was Brazil, whose plantation and mining economy was built in large part on the labour of the enslaved who survived the crossing — a sustained system of bondage, terror and death whose consequences still echo across Angola and the African diaspora.

events.title: Portuguese found Luanda
events.summary: Paulo Dias de Novais established São Paulo da Assunção de Loanda on the Angolan coast, creating the base from which Portuguese power and the slave trade would expand.

events.title: Founding of Benguela
events.summary: The Portuguese established the port of Benguela to the south, which would become a second major outlet for the export of enslaved Africans to the Americas.

events.title: Njinga becomes queen of Ndongo
events.summary: After leading a diplomatic mission to the Portuguese in 1621, Njinga Mbande took the throne of Ndongo, beginning a decades-long struggle against Portuguese expansion.

events.title: Njinga rules from Matamba
events.summary: Driven from Ndongo by the Portuguese, Njinga rebuilt her power in the eastern kingdom of Matamba, from which she resisted, raided and negotiated with the colonisers until peace in 1656.

events.title: Atlantic slave trade at its height
events.summary: Luanda became the leading slave-trading port of the entire Atlantic, funnelling vast numbers of captives seized in interior wars to the Americas, overwhelmingly to Brazil.

figures.name: Njinga (Nzinga) Mbande
figures.blurb: Ruler whose diplomatic skill and decades of armed resistance against the Portuguese made her the most celebrated figure of early Angolan history, remembered as a symbol of African resistance.

pullquote.text: Close to half of all Africans deported across the Atlantic came from the West Central African region centred on Angola, with Brazil the overwhelming destination.
pullquote.attribution: Estimates from the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database

eras.title: The slow conquest of the interior
eras.standfirst: For centuries Portuguese control scarcely reached beyond the coast and trade routes; only after the Berlin Conference did a slow, violent conquest bring the Ovimbundu, Lunda and other interior peoples under colonial rule by the early twentieth century.
body: Despite Luanda's age, Portuguese authority remained for centuries confined to the coast and a thin penetration along the trade routes. The interior was a world of independent kingdoms — the Ovimbundu of the highlands, the Lunda states of the east, the Kwanhama and other peoples of the south — that traded with the coast, including in enslaved people, but governed themselves. The transatlantic slave trade was formally abolished by Portugal in stages in the nineteenth century, though it persisted illegally and gave way to other forms of forced and contract labour.

The European 'Scramble for Africa' transformed this picture. The Berlin Conference of 1884–1885 obliged Portugal to demonstrate 'effective occupation' to hold its claims, triggering a series of military campaigns to subdue the interior. Between roughly 1890 and 1904 Portuguese forces conquered the Ovimbundu kingdoms of the central highlands, while the Lunda states to the east had already been weakened by Chokwe expansion before being absorbed. Resistance was fierce: in the south, fighting against the Kwanhama (Ovambo) culminated in heavy clashes such as the Battle of the Cunene in 1904.

Effective Portuguese control over the whole territory was achieved only in the first decades of the twentieth century, with 'pacification' campaigns continuing into the 1920s. Colonial rule rested on coerced labour, a rigid racial hierarchy and the export of resources, with little investment in the African majority. Discontent simmered under this system for decades before erupting into open revolt in the second half of the century.

events.title: Portugal abolishes the slave trade
events.summary: Portugal formally outlawed the transatlantic slave trade, ending Luanda's centuries as a slaving port, though clandestine traffic and later forced labour persisted in Angola.

events.title: Berlin Conference and the Scramble
events.summary: The Berlin Conference required Portugal to demonstrate effective occupation of Angola, spurring the military campaigns that would subdue the independent interior kingdoms.

events.title: Conquest of the Ovimbundu kingdoms
events.summary: Portuguese forces conquered the Ovimbundu kingdoms of the central highlands, bringing one of Angola's largest peoples under colonial control.

events.title: Battle of the Cunene
events.summary: Heavy fighting against the Kwanhama (Ovambo) in the south marked the violent and protracted character of the Portuguese conquest of Angola's interior frontier.

events.title: Effective occupation completed
events.summary: Only in the early twentieth century, with pacification campaigns continuing into the 1920s, did Portugal establish effective control over the whole of Angola.

pullquote.text: Despite three centuries of Portuguese presence on the coast, the interior of Angola was subdued only in the early twentieth century.

eras.title: Revolt, liberation movements and independence
eras.standfirst: Armed revolt broke out in 1961 and was carried forward by three rival movements — the MPLA, the FNLA and UNITA — until Portugal's 1974 revolution opened the way to independence under the MPLA on 11 November 1975.
body: Anti-colonial resistance turned to armed struggle in 1961. In January, cotton workers in the Baixa de Cassanje region of Malanje rose against forced labour and were crushed with great violence. On 4 February 1961 militants linked to the MPLA attacked a prison in Luanda, an act later commemorated as the start of the armed struggle, and on 15 March the União das Populações de Angola (UPA, later part of the FNLA) launched a major uprising in the northern Bakongo coffee region. Portugal responded with a brutal counter-insurgency, and the war ground on for over a decade.

Three rival liberation movements came to dominate the struggle, divided by region, ethnicity and ideology. The Marxist MPLA, led by the poet and physician Agostinho Neto, was based in Luanda among the Mbundu and the urban intelligentsia and backed by the Soviet bloc and Cuba. The National Front for the Liberation of Angola (FNLA), led by Holden Roberto, drew on the Bakongo north and on support from Zaire and the United States. The National Union for the Total Independence of Angola (UNITA), founded by Jonas Savimbi in 1966, was rooted among the Ovimbundu of the central highlands.

The deadlock broke in Portugal itself. The Carnation Revolution of 25 April 1974 overthrew the Lisbon dictatorship, and the new government moved to decolonise. In January 1975 the three movements and Portugal signed the Alvor Agreement, providing for a transitional government and independence, but mutual distrust soon collapsed into fighting between them. As the Portuguese withdrew, the MPLA proclaimed the People's Republic of Angola in Luanda on 11 November 1975, with Agostinho Neto as its first president, even as the rival movements declared their own claims.

events.title: Revolt breaks out
events.summary: The Baixa de Cassanje cotton workers' revolt, the 4 February attack on a Luanda prison, and the UPA's 15 March uprising in the north marked the start of Angola's war of independence against Portugal.

events.title: Founding of UNITA
events.summary: Jonas Savimbi founded the National Union for the Total Independence of Angola, drawing support from the Ovimbundu and creating the third of the rival liberation movements alongside the MPLA and FNLA.

events.title: Carnation Revolution in Portugal
events.summary: A military coup overthrew Portugal's dictatorship, and the new government moved to decolonise, opening the path to Angolan independence.

events.title: Alvor Agreement
events.summary: Portugal, the MPLA, the FNLA and UNITA signed the Alvor Agreement, providing for a transitional government and independence, but trust between the movements quickly collapsed into open conflict.

events.title: Independence proclaimed
events.summary: As the Portuguese departed, the MPLA proclaimed the People's Republic of Angola in Luanda under Agostinho Neto, marking the country's independence amid a contest with the rival movements.

figures.name: Agostinho Neto
figures.role: Independence leader and first President of Angola
figures.blurb: Poet, physician and leader of the MPLA who proclaimed Angola's independence on 11 November 1975 and served as its first president until his death in 1979.

figures.name: Jonas Savimbi
figures.blurb: Founder of UNITA in 1966 and its leader through the war of independence and the long civil war, in which he fought the MPLA with Western and South African backing until his death in 2002.

pullquote.text: Three rival movements — the MPLA, the FNLA and UNITA — fought the same colonial war, then turned their guns on one another at independence.

eras.title: The long civil war
eras.standfirst: From the moment of independence, Angola was plunged into a twenty-seven-year civil war between the MPLA government and Savimbi's UNITA — a Cold War proxy conflict drawing in Cuban, Soviet, South African and United States forces — that ended only with Savimbi's death in 2002.
body: Independence brought not peace but a civil war that would last until 2002. The MPLA government in Luanda, led after Neto's death in 1979 by José Eduardo dos Santos, fought UNITA under Jonas Savimbi for control of the country. The conflict became a Cold War battleground: the Soviet Union and Cuba backed the MPLA, with Cuban troops arriving in force from late 1975 in Operation Carlota and eventually numbering tens of thousands, while apartheid South Africa and the United States supported UNITA, and South African forces repeatedly invaded the south.

The fighting passed through distinct phases. A first long period from 1975 to 1991 included the massive set-piece confrontation around Cuito Cuanavale in 1987–1988, where Cuban, Angolan and South African armies clashed and which helped pave the way to a settlement. The 1988 New York Accords arranged the withdrawal of Cuban and South African troops and linked Angola's war to Namibian independence. The Bicesse Accords of 1991 brought a ceasefire and multiparty elections in 1992, but when Savimbi rejected the result, war resumed. A further attempt at peace, the Lusaka Protocol of 1994, also broke down, and heavy fighting returned from 1998.

The war was finally ended not by a treaty but by Savimbi's death: he was killed in an army ambush on 22 February 2002, after which UNITA and the government swiftly agreed a ceasefire formalised in the Luena Memorandum of 4 April 2002. Estimates of the dead over the twenty-seven years range from around 500,000 to as many as 800,000, with more than a million people displaced and much of the country sown with landmines. Angola emerged from the longest war in its history exhausted but at last at peace.

events.title: Cold War intervention
events.summary: As civil war erupted, South Africa invaded the south and Cuba sent troops to support the MPLA, turning Angola into a major proxy battleground between the superpower blocs.

events.title: Battle of Cuito Cuanavale
events.summary: Cuban, Angolan and South African forces fought a major conventional battle around Cuito Cuanavale, a turning point that helped lead to negotiated foreign troop withdrawals.

events.title: Bicesse Accords
events.summary: A ceasefire under the Bicesse Accords legalised UNITA as a party and set the stage for multiparty elections, briefly raising hopes of an end to the war.

events.title: Disputed elections and renewed war
events.summary: The MPLA's dos Santos led UNITA's Savimbi in Angola's first multiparty elections, but Savimbi rejected the result and returned to war, plunging the country back into conflict.

events.title: Death of Savimbi ends the war
events.summary: Jonas Savimbi was killed in an ambush by government forces; within weeks UNITA and the MPLA signed the Luena Memorandum, ending the twenty-seven-year civil war.

figures.name: José Eduardo dos Santos
figures.blurb: Succeeded Neto as MPLA leader and president in 1979, led the government through the civil war to victory in 2002, and ruled for thirty-eight years before stepping down in 2017.

pullquote.text: Estimates of the dead over the twenty-seven-year war range from around 500,000 to as many as 800,000, with more than a million displaced.
pullquote.attribution: Estimates of the Angolan Civil War's toll

eras.title: Peace and oil-rich modern Angola
eras.standfirst: Since 2002 Angola has rebuilt on the strength of its vast oil wealth, remaining under MPLA rule, with a long-delayed leadership transition in 2017 from José Eduardo dos Santos to João Lourenço.
body: Peace in 2002 allowed Angola to begin reconstruction after decades of war. Soaring oil production and high prices in the 2000s drove rapid economic growth, financing the rebuilding of cities, roads and railways, though the wealth was unevenly shared and corruption was widespread. The MPLA, in power since independence, consolidated its dominance, and José Eduardo dos Santos remained president, one of Africa's longest-serving leaders.

Angola became one of Africa's leading oil producers and a member of OPEC, with crude oil providing the overwhelming share of its exports and government revenue. This dependence left the economy exposed to the swings of the global oil price, and the slump from 2014 brought recession, debt and austerity, exposing the costs of a narrow, resource-based economy and the inequality that had grown alongside the boom.

In 2017 dos Santos stepped down after thirty-eight years, and his chosen successor, the former defence minister João Lourenço, became president while the MPLA retained power. Lourenço launched anti-corruption measures, including investigations into members of the dos Santos family, and sought to diversify the economy away from oil. Angola today remains an MPLA-led republic working to convert its mineral wealth into broader development while confronting the legacies of its long and violent past.

events.title: Reconstruction and the oil boom
events.summary: Peace allowed Angola to rebuild on the back of surging oil revenues, driving rapid growth in the 2000s amid persistent inequality and corruption.

events.title: Angola joins OPEC
events.summary: Angola became a member of OPEC, underscoring its standing as one of Africa's leading oil producers and the centrality of crude oil to its economy.

events.title: Lourenço succeeds dos Santos
events.summary: After thirty-eight years in power, José Eduardo dos Santos stepped down and João Lourenço became president, with the MPLA retaining control of the government.

figures.name: João Lourenço
figures.blurb: Former defence minister who succeeded dos Santos in 2017, launching anti-corruption drives and efforts to diversify Angola's oil-dependent economy while keeping the MPLA in power.

pullquote.text: Crude oil provides the overwhelming share of Angola's exports and revenue, leaving the economy exposed to every swing of the global price.

figures.name: Njinga (Nzinga) Mbande
figures.blurb: The most celebrated figure of early Angolan history, whose diplomacy and decades of armed resistance against the Portuguese made her an enduring symbol of African resistance, remembered as 'Mother of Angola'.

figures.name: Agostinho Neto
figures.blurb: Poet, physician and MPLA leader who proclaimed Angolan independence on 11 November 1975 and became the new nation's first president.
